sentences.text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That took the people, not just the Polish people, the Jewish people.
They bring people from Europe and the French people.
They put them by the walls, clean up, call them up, and that's all.
Plaszow, that's-- this Plaszow was the same like today.
You've got in Africa what's going on here.
I remember when I was supposed to go, when they brought a couple of people to shoot them.
I was, myself, in the grave to kill.
They took, the SS, took it.
You go right over there with everybody.
There'd be a lot of-- young people, old people.
Most of them, you know, who helped the Germans, the Ukraine, Ukraine people.
Oh, they was happy to shoot a Jew, oh!
They put on all of their new uniforms.
Here come another German, tell them get out from over here.
You see, they're not-- me, my brother, both of us was by the wall.
Come another SS man, tell him, get out.
What are you doing over here, both of you?
He hollered to us, get out!
It's not your business to stay over here.
It's even for the Nazis, the [INAUDIBLE] Nazi.
They have to go with them because they're the Germany, the German.
If they got another, they're going.
I was already-- they tell me come on.
Me and my brother, both of us, we go together, not separated.
We sat over there on the side, me and my brother by the wall.
Comes the fuhrer from the German army tell them-- in German-- what are you doing over here?
Tell them to send me over here.
He take out the back wall back to the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a big story to take about them.
So is that all there is to that story?
He come over there to the Plaszow.
He talk with the lagerfuhrer, Goeth.
Oh boy, oh, boy, that guy was.
When he come to us, to Brunnlitz, Schindler told them what are you looking over here?
What do you think, you're important?
Picture after the end of war, they look after him.
The SS, themselves, they look after him.
Oh, it was a big steal.
They steal everything from the Jewish people.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I wish never come again.
No, no, I don't wish that.
Me, my brother, together, go to Brunnlitz.
They tell us where to go.
Schindler, himself, go is go with the people.
He go with some in the train.
Schindler-- may he rest in peace-- he not go because of the Jews.
You know, the Jewish people got the real one.
And I can't tell you because I don't remember him.
I remember when the war time.
Yeah, he walked through the train.
He not say nothing, no, no.
He talk a little, and after this, he's go, take the car.
I know of his wife too.
And he after that, after the train.
We get off on the train.
We go to make a ready for the beds.
In Plaszow, we never got food.
Why would they cook over there?
That's a long time, 50 years ago.
Plaszow you got to sleep on the floor, in a bed, a different kind of bed, with wood.
Go sit one, the bottom, another on the top.
No he-- what he was before, I don't know.
And I can't tell you about that.
He saved a lot of people.
He saved 3,000 people. 3,000 people, not two or three.
What he was before, I don't know.
And I can't tell you because I don't know him.
I know what I see in the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know what ammunition is?
No, no they not make in Plaszow.
I put it in the-- when they were about ready, put in the oven.
And like this, like they make over here.
When it was ready, I take them out.
I put it in, on that side and that's all.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was never happy like that.
Oh, in Brunnlitz, we got up seven o'clock, sometimes six o'clock.
And after this, you go to the machine.
They got the cafeteria over there.
See, but then sometimes was going outside to work.
And after this, they come, somebody from the Germans tell them, hey, not let them out.
And they ask, what's he doing?
Because, as you see, there was care, when the Czechoslovakian come pick at us up and then take away in front of the Germans.
And then I go no more.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The grapes from the hills.
They got the sun over there.
You see, where we come over there, the SS talked to the Czechoslovakian people.
The Czechoslovakian people, they work together.
This-- it's up to the people.
If they want to work, they go right.
They look out for the Germans.
They didn't make a sabotage over there.
Even the German themselves are scared.
A lot of Germans go away.
They want to be over there.
It was-- all of it was the same-- the same.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He saved about 5,000 people, international.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He saved people from other places, and other camps and brought them over here to give them to eat.
He say that, you know what?
I've got more people to come over here.
Sure, he give everybody-- even he was an SS man.
And that was day, he give for everybody.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When I was in Israel, I was by his grave.
See, the Jewish make it for him.
He was a savior for 3,000 Jewish people.
Even he was a Gestapo, SS.
He no save for the Jews.
No, he was a good man.
He died not about three, four years ago in New York.
You in the camp, all you can do find out where they are.
When I come finished, after the war.
Oh no, I not see him.
He cry-- they told me, he cry.
You see, before the Russian come over, about two weeks ago, before the Russian supposed to come to Brunnlitz to take over.
They're telling the people, hey, make a party, something for you.
Get out because if they catch you, they'll kill you.
Well, they collect their money, something to make something to give him a present.
Because he wants to be with the people.
Even though he was a Gestapo, an SS man, he liked be the people.
Oh, he's not more over here.
See, but two weeks before he left, the Russians come over here.
And if the Russians catch him, they would have killed him.
The Nazis come over, they opened up the doors.
I told them, how we can go home?
No we try train for you.
And two days later, you go out back home.
The Russian like I gave them.
First, all the kind Russian, they're good, they're bad.
Go, go-- when they come time-- go home.
Me, and my brother, we go on the train.
It takes a couple days, three, four days.
See, from Czechoslovakia, they have to go to someplace else, change the train.
I try, maybe, I'll find somebody.
And then I was going to my hometown, my house, my house where I was living.
And they come out, hey, Moses, Moses, Moses.
I live over there in a hotel.
We go to the Jewish organization.
When I got family in Krakow, I will stay.
When I come over to my family, let's all go to Russia.
One sister was in a Nazi camp too, the younger sister.
No, not-- one sister was in Krakow when I was coming, yeah.
She would live over there where she working.
And after this, we go someplace, I get married in Germany.
I supposed to go to-- here I want to go this time to Israel.
And I would belong to the organization.
They tell me, OK, you want to go?
In Germany, I find my wife.
Oh, about a year or two.
I want to go to Israel.
I just look at other people going.
When I go someplace, never sleep.
She's from-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] she far from east-- I call them.
And I met her in Germany.
The first time when I saw her I talked to my friend, you see the women that goes over there with the steps?
A couple days later, I told her come on.
I just look her in the face, thought, she's a good person.
I got her over here, yeah.
He come over, the rabbi, and I get married.
I think I got the marriage too.
That's how we get like that.
I live with her about 40 years.
And then I go back home.
What I can get at, I catched.
Oh no, we first when we come, I work Gerber's baby food.
I get-- when I married her, I go back to Germany.
And after Germany, I come over here.
You see, Israel this time was in war.
And I want to go Israel.
Israel told me, no, you no can go.
Because my wife got the boy, the son.
I tell them, what I can do?
I don't want to be over here.
Well, make application to the American's Council down at the Israeli council.
Israeli Council told me, you want in?
I came in and 10 minutes you got the passport.
My wife told me no, not yet.
I go to the American Council.
I can not give you because you are a communist.
I get up in the Nazi camp, and I'm a communist?
I go by, Israeli called me come on.
No, what's happened, I have to wait another year.
And after this, they give me, they call me up.
Come on, I give you the passport--
A lot of people like that were like that.
Not that-- she never got married before, no.
From Germany, I go to Italy.
In Italy, I live in a private house.
Because I like it better from Germany go to Italy.
And Italy it was better to go over here.
It's a long time, about 50 years.
See I was supposed to come over here before.
[INAUDIBLE] didn't want to give me that part.
He told me I'm a communist.
